Source_Species.reason: The paper studies Arabidopsis and repeatedly refers to Arabidopsis thaliana.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly stated in the title/introduction/methods.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is the wild-type accession used, stated in Methods.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly the experimental species.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Methods list Col-0 and tt19-8; Results identify tt19-8 as SALK_105779C, while Methods list SALK_105779. The combined notation reflects paper content.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: TT19 is stated to be required for anthocyanin accumulation and proanthocyanidin pigments in the seed coat.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins are discussed as flavonoid pigments/pathway products.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Anthocyanins are described as transported to vacuoles; TT19 affects anthocyanin accumulation in vegetative organs and PA pigmentation in seed coat.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Anthocyanins are said to furnish a visual output of stress/metabolic status; PAs are described as seed coat pigments.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: TT19 is explicitly described as a glutathione S-transferase and functional homolog of maize BZ2.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The introduction states that TT19 shows a broad subcellular distribution.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show tt19-8 has severe anthocyanin deficiency, reduced seed coat PA pigmentation, and that rdr6/sgs3/dcl4 mutations suppress the vegetative anthocyanin defect but not the seed-coat PA defect.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods/results are directly used: EMS suppressor screen, mutant phenotyping, double mutant validation, anthocyanin quantification, and flavonoid profiling.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements are directly supported: tt19-8 has about 10–11% wild-type anthocyanin; suppressors restore vegetative but not seed coat phenotype; Discussion states enough anthocyanin can accumulate without TT19 when flux is increased.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: AIC (3% sucrose, strong/continuous light) is defined in Methods/Results, and suppressor effects on soil-grown plants are also reported.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper includes RNA-seq, small RNA-seq, whole-genome resequencing, untargeted metabolomics, and LC-MS/MS flavonoid profiling, with accession numbers in Data availability.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Loss of TT19 is shown to cause strong seedling anthocyanin deficiency and reduced seed coat PA pigmentation.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a plausible interpretation, but explicit breeding/engineering value for TT19 is not directly tested or stated in Results.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly says TT19 is proposed to bind and stabilize anthocyanins and participate in vacuolar sequestration, but the exact mechanism remains unclear.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three numerical/conceptual points are directly stated in Results/Discussion.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Fig. 2 and text show high PAP1 in PAP1-D and elevated PAP1 in RDR6-related suppressor backgrounds.
